questions.question: Which of the following BEST defines commerce?

options.A: The process of manufacturing goods for sale
options.B: All activities involved in the exchange of goods and services and the aids that facilitate exchange
options.C: The transportation of goods from one location to another
options.D: The production of raw materials for industry

questions.explanation: Commerce covers trade (buying and selling) plus all aids to trade such as banking, insurance, transport, warehousing, advertising, and communication.

questions.question: Home trade refers to

options.A: Trade carried out within the boundaries of a country
options.B: Trade between two neighbouring countries
options.C: Trade restricted to households only
options.D: Online trading from home

questions.explanation: Home trade (domestic/internal trade) is buying and selling of goods and services within the borders of a country.

questions.question: Entrepôt trade is best described as

options.A: Trade in which goods are imported for immediate consumption
options.B: The importation of goods for re-export to other countries
options.C: Trade conducted only between developed nations
options.D: The export of raw materials by developing countries

questions.explanation: Entrepôt trade involves importing goods into a country and re-exporting them, often after processing or re-packaging. Singapore and Dubai are classic entrepôt centres.

questions.question: A favourable balance of trade occurs when

options.A: Imports exceed exports
options.B: Exports exceed imports
options.C: Exports and imports are equal
options.D: The government eliminates all import tariffs

questions.explanation: A favourable (positive) balance of trade means the value of exports exceeds the value of imports, resulting in a trade surplus.

questions.question: An import quota is

options.A: A tax placed on imported goods
options.B: A physical limit on the quantity of goods that may be imported
options.C: A ban on all imports from a specific country
options.D: A subsidy given to domestic producers

questions.explanation: An import quota is a quantitative restriction setting a maximum limit on the amount of a specific good that can be imported during a given period.

questions.question: A tariff is BEST described as

options.A: A government subsidy on exported goods
options.B: A tax imposed on imported goods
options.C: A ban on trade with a specific country
options.D: A licence granted to importers

questions.explanation: A tariff is a tax levied on imported goods. It raises the price of imports, making domestic goods more competitive and generating government revenue.

questions.question: Dumping in international trade occurs when

options.A: A country bans the export of its goods to another country
options.B: A country exports goods at a price below their cost of production
options.C: A country floods its domestic market with foreign goods
options.D: A country imposes very high tariffs on imported goods

questions.explanation: Dumping is selling exported goods below cost or below domestic prices, potentially harming the industries of the importing country.

questions.question: An embargo is a trade policy that

options.A: Reduces import duties on specified goods
options.B: Completely bans trade in certain goods or with certain countries
options.C: Limits the quantity of goods that can be imported
options.D: Provides subsidies to domestic exporters

questions.explanation: An embargo is an official ban on trade with a specific country or on specific goods, often used for political or security reasons.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an aid to trade?

options.A: Banking
options.B: Insurance
options.C: Manufacturing
options.D: Warehousing

questions.explanation: Aids to trade include banking, insurance, transportation, warehousing, advertising, and communication. Manufacturing is an industrial activity, not an aid to trade.

questions.question: Foreign trade involves

options.A: Trade between two firms in the same city
options.B: Exchange of goods and services between two or more countries
options.C: Buying goods from foreign companies only
options.D: Selling goods to other states within the same country

questions.explanation: Foreign trade (international trade) is the exchange of goods and services across national borders, involving import, export, and entrepôt trade.

questions.question: A wholesaler is BEST described as

options.A: A trader who sells goods directly to the final consumer
options.B: A trader who buys in bulk from producers and sells in smaller quantities to retailers
options.C: A trader who produces and sells goods to retailers
options.D: A government agency that controls the supply of goods

questions.explanation: A wholesaler is a middleman who buys large quantities from manufacturers and sells smaller lots to retailers, bridging the gap between producers and retailers.

questions.question: Which of the following is a function of the wholesaler?

options.A: Selling goods directly to consumers at retail prices
options.B: Breaking bulk — dividing large consignments into smaller quantities
options.C: Manufacturing goods for the home market
options.D: Setting prices for all goods in the economy

questions.explanation: Breaking bulk is a key wholesaler function — buying in large quantities from manufacturers and re-selling in smaller, manageable quantities to retailers.

questions.question: A cash-and-carry wholesaler differs from a traditional wholesaler in that

options.A: It offers credit facilities to all customers
options.B: Retailers pay cash and collect their own goods without delivery
options.C: It only sells perishable goods
options.D: It sells goods below cost price

questions.explanation: In cash-and-carry wholesale, retailers visit the warehouse, select goods, pay cash on the spot, and transport the goods themselves. No credit or delivery is provided.

questions.question: When a wholesaler is eliminated from the chain of distribution, this is known as

options.A: Vertical integration
options.B: Disintermediation (cutting out the middleman)
options.C: Horizontal integration
options.D: Franchising

questions.explanation: Disintermediation occurs when manufacturers sell directly to retailers or consumers, bypassing the wholesaler.

questions.question: A retailer is a trader who

options.A: Buys and sells goods in large quantities
options.B: Sells goods directly to the final consumer
options.C: Only sells goods on credit
options.D: Imports goods from foreign countries for sale

questions.explanation: A retailer is the last link in the chain of distribution, selling goods in small quantities directly to the final consumer.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a large-scale retailer?

options.A: A roadside food vendor
options.B: A market trader
options.C: A supermarket
options.D: A door-to-door salesman

questions.explanation: Large-scale retailers include supermarkets, department stores, chain stores, and hypermarkets, operating with significant capital and wide product variety.

questions.question: A department store is divided into sections selling different categories of goods under one roof. This makes it different from a supermarket which

options.A: Sells goods at wholesale prices
options.B: Mainly sells food and household goods under one roof
options.C: Only operates as a chain store
options.D: Is always government owned

questions.explanation: A department store has sections for different goods (clothing, furniture, electronics, etc.). Supermarkets mainly stock food and household goods.

questions.question: Chain stores are retail outlets that

options.A: Are owned by different individual traders using the same name
options.B: Are multiple branches of the same organisation under central ownership
options.C: Only sell goods on instalment plans
options.D: Operate without fixed premises

questions.explanation: Chain stores (multiple shops) are a group of retail outlets owned and controlled by the same company sharing the same name, layout, and policies.

questions.question: Mail order retailing means

options.A: Selling goods through advertisements and delivering to buyers by post or courier
options.B: Selling goods through retail stores located in post offices
options.C: Ordering goods from a wholesaler using the postal service
options.D: Selling goods at market stalls with home delivery

questions.explanation: Mail order is a retail method where goods are advertised in catalogues or media, customers order remotely, and goods are delivered directly to the buyer.

questions.question: Vending machines represent which type of retailing?

options.A: Itinerant trading
options.B: Automatic retailing
options.C: Mail order retailing
options.D: Market trading

questions.explanation: Vending machines are examples of automatic retailing — operating 24/7 without human assistance, dispensing goods in exchange for payment.

questions.question: A consumer cooperative society is formed mainly to

options.A: Maximise profits for shareholders
options.B: Enable members to buy goods at lower prices by eliminating middlemen
options.C: Provide employment for government workers
options.D: Finance agricultural production

questions.explanation: Consumer cooperatives pool resources to buy goods in bulk, eliminate middlemen, and sell to members at reduced prices. Surpluses are shared as dividends based on patronage.

questions.question: The dividend paid to members of a cooperative society is based on

options.A: The amount of shares each member holds
options.B: Each member's purchases (patronage)
options.C: The length of membership
options.D: Members' contributions to the management committee

questions.explanation: In a cooperative, the surplus (dividend) is distributed based on members' purchases or patronage, not shareholding. This distinguishes cooperatives from ordinary companies.

questions.question: The shortest channel of distribution is

options.A: Producer → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer
options.B: Producer → Agent → Retailer → Consumer
options.C: Producer → Consumer
options.D: Producer → Retailer → Consumer

questions.explanation: Producer → Consumer (direct selling) is the shortest channel with no middlemen, e.g., farm gate sales, factory shops, direct online sales.

questions.question: An agent in the channel of distribution is best described as

options.A: A trader who buys and owns goods before selling them
options.B: A person who acts on behalf of a principal to buy or sell goods without taking title
options.C: A government-appointed trader who controls prices
options.D: A retailer who sells only on credit

questions.explanation: An agent facilitates the sale of goods on behalf of a principal without taking ownership. The agent earns a commission.

questions.question: A del credere agent differs from other agents in that

options.A: He charges no commission
options.B: He guarantees payment to the principal if the buyer defaults
options.C: He can only sell in the domestic market
options.D: He must buy the goods before selling them

questions.explanation: A del credere agent assumes the risk of non-payment by buyers, providing a guarantee to the principal in exchange for a higher commission.

questions.question: A broker in trade differs from a factor in that a broker

options.A: Takes possession of goods and sells in their own name
options.B: Arranges deals between buyers and sellers without taking possession of the goods
options.C: Provides insurance for goods in transit
options.D: Sets market prices on behalf of the principal

questions.explanation: A broker acts as intermediary between buyers and sellers without taking title to or physical possession of goods, earning a commission or brokerage fee.

questions.question: A franchise arrangement is best described as

options.A: A government licence to operate a business
options.B: An arrangement where a franchisor grants a franchisee the right to use its brand and systems
options.C: A partnership between a foreign and local company
options.D: A government subsidy given to small businesses

questions.explanation: In a franchise, the franchisor grants the franchisee the right to operate using its established brand, products, and business systems in exchange for fees and royalties (e.g., KFC, McDonald's).

questions.question: Which document does a buyer send to a seller to request specific goods?

options.A: Invoice
options.B: Receipt
options.C: Purchase order (order form)
options.D: Credit note

questions.explanation: A purchase order is sent by the buyer to the seller specifying the goods required, quantity, price, and delivery terms.

questions.question: A proforma invoice is used to

options.A: Demand immediate payment from a debtor
options.B: Show the price of goods before a formal order is placed
options.C: Record goods returned by the buyer
options.D: Acknowledge receipt of payment

questions.explanation: A proforma invoice is a preliminary document showing the seller's prices and terms before the actual transaction. It is used for quotation or advance payment requests.

questions.question: An invoice is a document that

options.A: Is sent by the buyer to acknowledge receipt of goods
options.B: Is sent by the seller showing details and amounts owed for goods supplied
options.C: Is used to return faulty goods to the supplier
options.D: Confirms goods have been insured during transit

questions.explanation: An invoice is a commercial document issued by the seller to the buyer detailing goods supplied, quantity, price, and total amount owed.

questions.question: A credit note is issued by the seller to the buyer when

options.A: The buyer requests additional goods
options.B: Goods are returned or the buyer was overcharged
options.C: The buyer defaults on payment
options.D: New goods are dispatched to the buyer

questions.explanation: A credit note reduces the amount owed by the buyer — issued when goods are returned, incorrect goods were sent, or the buyer was overcharged.

questions.question: A debit note is sent by the seller to the buyer when

options.A: The seller wants to reduce the buyer's debt
options.B: The buyer was undercharged or additional charges are owed
options.C: The seller acknowledges safe receipt of payment
options.D: The buyer cancels an order

questions.explanation: A debit note informs the buyer that their account has been debited — used when the seller undercharged or additional charges apply.

questions.question: A statement of account is prepared by the seller to show

options.A: The buyer's credit rating
options.B: All transactions with the buyer over a period and the balance outstanding
options.C: The bank balance of the seller
options.D: The company's annual tax obligations

questions.explanation: A statement of account is sent by the seller showing all invoices, credit notes, payments received, and the balance outstanding over a period (usually monthly).

questions.question: Which document accompanies goods when they are dispatched to the buyer?

options.A: Statement of account
options.B: Delivery note
options.C: Credit note
options.D: Letter of credit

questions.explanation: A delivery note accompanies goods and describes the items being delivered. The buyer signs and returns a copy to confirm receipt.

questions.question: An enquiry in a business transaction is sent by

options.A: The seller to offer goods for sale
options.B: The buyer to a potential seller to request information about goods and prices
options.C: The bank to a customer about an overdrawn account
options.D: The customs authority to an importer

questions.explanation: An enquiry is sent by the potential buyer to sellers requesting details about goods — availability, prices, terms, and delivery conditions.

questions.question: A quotation is sent by the seller to

options.A: Demand payment for goods already supplied
options.B: State the price and conditions under which the seller is willing to supply goods
options.C: Acknowledge receipt of payment
options.D: Confirm that goods have been dispatched

questions.explanation: A quotation is sent in response to a buyer's enquiry, providing detailed information about prices, terms of sale, discounts, and delivery conditions.

questions.question: A receipt is issued by

options.A: The buyer to confirm that goods have been ordered
options.B: The seller to acknowledge that payment has been received
options.C: The bank to confirm a cheque has been cleared
options.D: The transport company to confirm safe delivery of goods

questions.explanation: A receipt is a written acknowledgement issued by the seller confirming that payment has been received from the buyer.

questions.question: A commercial bank primarily makes its profit by

options.A: Charging fees for government services
options.B: Accepting deposits and lending money at a higher interest rate
options.C: Selling shares to the public
options.D: Printing money on behalf of the government

questions.explanation: Commercial banks earn profit mainly from the interest spread — accepting deposits at lower rates and lending money at higher rates.

questions.question: A current account differs from a savings account in that

options.A: A current account earns higher interest
options.B: A current account allows unlimited withdrawals using cheques but usually pays no interest
options.C: Only businesses can open a current account
options.D: A savings account does not require a minimum balance

questions.explanation: A current (checking) account is designed for frequent transactions with unlimited withdrawals via cheques or debit cards. It generally pays no or minimal interest.

questions.question: A cheque is BEST described as

options.A: A document issued by the central bank to regulate money supply
options.B: A written order addressed to a bank to pay a specified sum to a named person or bearer
options.C: A receipt issued by a bank when a deposit is made
options.D: A promissory note issued by a trader

questions.explanation: A cheque is a written instruction from an account holder directing their bank to pay a specified amount to a named person or bearer.

questions.question: A crossed cheque means

options.A: The cheque has expired
options.B: The cheque can only be deposited into a bank account and cannot be cashed over the counter
options.C: The cheque has been returned unpaid
options.D: The cheque has been signed by two signatories

questions.explanation: A crossed cheque has two parallel lines across its face. It cannot be cashed directly — it must be deposited into a bank account, improving security.

questions.question: An overdraft facility allows a current account holder to

options.A: Earn extra interest on their deposits
options.B: Withdraw more money than their account balance up to an agreed limit
options.C: Transfer funds to another bank without charges
options.D: Receive government social welfare payments

questions.explanation: An overdraft allows a bank customer to withdraw funds in excess of their account balance up to an agreed limit. Interest is charged on the amount overdrawn.

questions.question: The central bank of a country performs which of the following functions?

options.A: Accepting deposits from the general public
options.B: Acting as the banker to the government and controlling the money supply
options.C: Selling goods to government ministries
options.D: Insuring deposits of commercial bank customers

questions.explanation: The central bank acts as the government's banker, controls money supply and credit, issues currency, regulates commercial banks, and acts as lender of last resort.

questions.question: A fixed deposit account earns a higher rate of interest because

options.A: It is the safest type of bank account
options.B: Money is locked away for a fixed period and cannot be withdrawn before maturity
options.C: It is only available to high net-worth individuals
options.D: The bank invests the deposits in the stock market

questions.explanation: A fixed deposit (term deposit) earns higher interest because the depositor commits money for a fixed period, giving the bank certainty for lending. Early withdrawal may incur penalties.

questions.question: A letter of credit in international trade is issued by

options.A: The exporter's shipping company
options.B: The importer's bank, guaranteeing payment to the exporter
options.C: The central bank of the exporting country
options.D: The chamber of commerce of the importing country

questions.explanation: A letter of credit is issued by the importer's bank, guaranteeing the exporter payment once specified conditions (e.g., presentation of shipping documents) are fulfilled.

questions.question: A promissory note is a written promise by

options.A: A bank promising to pay the bearer on demand
options.B: A debtor promising to pay a specific amount to a creditor at a fixed future date
options.C: A government agency promising to deliver goods
options.D: A seller promising to replace defective goods

questions.explanation: A promissory note is an unconditional written promise made by the debtor to pay a specified sum to the creditor on demand or at a specific future date.

questions.question: A bill of exchange is drawn by

options.A: The debtor (buyer) and addressed to the creditor
options.B: The creditor (seller) and addressed to the debtor, ordering payment at a future date
options.C: The central bank to regulate money supply
options.D: The importer and addressed to the exporter

questions.explanation: A bill of exchange is drawn by the creditor/seller and addressed to the debtor/buyer, ordering them to pay a specified sum on demand or at a future date.

questions.question: Bank reconciliation statement is prepared to

options.A: Show profits earned by the bank in a year
options.B: Explain differences between the bank statement balance and the cash book balance
options.C: Record all loans granted by the bank in a year
options.D: Show the number of customers served by a bank

questions.explanation: A bank reconciliation statement explains the differences between the company's cash book balance and the bank statement balance, due to items like unpresented cheques.

questions.question: A post-dated cheque is one that

options.A: Was issued in the past and has since expired
options.B: Bears a future date and cannot be presented to the bank until that date
options.C: Has been returned by the bank due to insufficient funds
options.D: Has been signed by two signatories

questions.explanation: A post-dated cheque carries a future date. The bank will not honour it until that date arrives.

questions.question: A stale cheque is one that

options.A: Was issued on old paper
options.B: Has not been presented to the bank within a reasonable period (usually 6 months) after its date
options.C: Contains errors and has been altered
options.D: Is signed by an unauthorised person

questions.explanation: A stale cheque has not been presented for payment within the stipulated period (typically six months). Banks refuse to honour stale cheques.

questions.question: The principle of indemnity in insurance states that

options.A: The insured must disclose all material facts
options.B: The insured must have a financial interest in the subject matter
options.C: The insured should be restored to the same financial position as before the loss, no better
options.D: The insurer can recover losses from a third party who caused the damage

questions.explanation: The principle of indemnity means insurance is not a means of profit — the insured is only compensated to the extent of actual financial loss, restoring them to their pre-loss position.

questions.question: The principle of insurable interest means that

options.A: The insured must benefit from the safety of the insured item and suffer from its destruction
options.B: Any person can insure any property regardless of ownership
options.C: The insurer must pay all claims regardless of cause
options.D: All properties must be insured for their full market value

questions.explanation: Insurable interest means the insured must have a financial stake — benefiting from the item's safety and suffering a financial loss if it is damaged or destroyed.

questions.question: The principle of utmost good faith (uberrima fides) requires that

options.A: Both the insurer and the insured must fully and honestly disclose all material facts
options.B: Only the insurer must disclose information
options.C: The insured need not reveal pre-existing conditions
options.D: The contract must be witnessed by a government official

questions.explanation: Utmost good faith requires both parties to voluntarily disclose all material facts relevant to the insurance contract. Non-disclosure can void the policy.

questions.question: Subrogation in insurance means

options.A: The insured can claim from multiple insurers for the same loss
options.B: After compensating the insured, the insurer takes over the right to recover losses from a third party
options.C: The insured transfers the policy to another person
options.D: The insurer divides the risk with other insurers

questions.explanation: Subrogation allows the insurer, after paying the insured's claim, to sue the third party responsible for the loss — preventing the insured from profiting twice.

questions.question: Contribution as a principle of insurance applies when

options.A: Several people contribute to one insurance fund
options.B: The same property is insured with more than one insurer
options.C: The insurer deducts a contribution from the insured before paying
options.D: A group of firms jointly insure a very large risk

questions.explanation: The principle of contribution applies when the same risk is covered by two or more insurers. Each insurer pays only their proportionate share of the loss.

questions.question: A premium in insurance refers to

options.A: The sum paid by the insurer to the insured in a claim
options.B: The regular payment made by the insured to the insurer in exchange for coverage
options.C: The excess amount the insured must pay before a claim is settled
options.D: The additional commission earned by the insurance broker

questions.explanation: A premium is the sum paid by the policyholder to the insurance company at regular intervals in exchange for insurance coverage.

questions.question: Life assurance differs from general insurance mainly in that

options.A: Life assurance is compulsory by law
options.B: The event covered (death) is certain to happen; only the timing is uncertain
options.C: Life assurance does not require payment of premiums
options.D: Life assurance is only available to business owners

questions.explanation: Life assurance covers death, which is certain to occur — only the timing is unknown. General insurance covers uncertain events (fire, theft) that may or may not happen.

questions.question: Which of the following risks is NOT normally insurable?

options.A: Fire damage to a building
options.B: Theft of a motor vehicle
options.C: Loss due to a fall in market prices
options.D: Death of a breadwinner

questions.explanation: Speculative risks (where there is a possibility of gain, like market price fluctuations) are not insurable. Only pure risks (where only loss can occur) are insurable.

questions.question: A third-party motor insurance policy covers

options.A: Damage to the insured's own vehicle
options.B: Injury or damage caused to other people and their property by the insured driver
options.C: Both the insured's vehicle and third party liabilities
options.D: Medical expenses of the insured driver

questions.explanation: Third-party insurance (the minimum required by law) covers only liabilities to other people — injuries or property damage caused to third parties by the insured driver.

questions.question: Reinsurance means

options.A: An insured taking out a second insurance policy on the same risk
options.B: An insurer passing part of a large risk to another insurer to spread the risk
options.C: A policy that is renewed after it expires
options.D: An insurance contract that covers more than one person

questions.explanation: Reinsurance is the practice of an insurer transferring part of its risk to another insurance company (reinsurer) to reduce exposure to large claims.

questions.question: Which mode of transport is most suitable for heavy and bulky goods over long distances at low cost within a country?

options.A: Air transport
options.B: Road transport
options.C: Rail transport
options.D: Pipeline transport

questions.explanation: Rail transport is best suited for carrying heavy, bulky goods (coal, minerals, machinery) over long distances cheaply and efficiently.

questions.question: A bill of lading is a document used in

options.A: Road transport only
options.B: Sea (ocean) transport
options.C: Air transport
options.D: Rail transport

questions.explanation: A bill of lading is issued by the shipping company to the shipper. It serves as a receipt for goods, evidence of the contract of carriage, and a document of title.

questions.question: An airway bill is to air transport as a bill of lading is to

options.A: Road transport
options.B: Rail transport
options.C: Sea transport
options.D: Pipeline transport

questions.explanation: An airway bill (air waybill) is the document for air cargo, just as a bill of lading is used for sea transport. Both serve as receipt, contract, and document of title.

questions.question: Which of the following is an advantage of air transport?

options.A: It is the cheapest form of transport
options.B: It is the fastest mode and suitable for perishable and high-value goods
options.C: It can carry the heaviest and most bulky goods
options.D: It is available in remote areas without infrastructure

questions.explanation: Air transport is the fastest mode, ideal for high-value, perishable, or urgently needed goods. Its main disadvantages are high cost and limited capacity.

questions.question: Pipeline transport is most commonly used to move

options.A: Packaged consumer goods
options.B: Liquid and gaseous substances such as oil and gas
options.C: Livestock and agricultural produce
options.D: Heavy machinery and equipment

questions.explanation: Pipelines are designed for continuous movement of liquids (crude oil, petroleum products) and gases (natural gas) over long distances.

questions.question: Containerisation in sea transport refers to

options.A: Storing goods in bonded warehouses at the seaport
options.B: Packing goods into standard-sized steel containers for efficient transport
options.C: Placing goods in sealed tanks for liquid transport
options.D: Covering goods with protective wrapping before shipping

questions.explanation: Containerisation involves packing goods into standardised intermodal containers transferable between ships, trucks, and trains, reducing handling costs and transit times.

questions.question: A freight note is a document that shows

options.A: The insurance premium for goods in transit
options.B: The charges for transporting goods
options.C: The weight and dimensions of goods to be exported
options.D: The customs duty payable on imported goods

questions.explanation: A freight note is issued by the carrier and details the charges for transporting the goods from one point to another.

questions.question: The primary function of a warehouse is to

options.A: Process and manufacture goods for sale
options.B: Store goods safely between production and consumption
options.C: Provide retail services to the final consumer
options.D: Transport goods from producers to retailers

questions.explanation: A warehouse provides safe storage of goods between the time of production and the time of consumption, bridging the time gap between supply and demand.

questions.question: A bonded warehouse is used to store

options.A: Frozen and perishable goods only
options.B: Imported goods on which customs duty has not yet been paid
options.C: Goods belonging to government ministries
options.D: Goods awaiting transportation to retailers

questions.explanation: A bonded warehouse stores imported goods on which import duty has not yet been paid. Goods are released only when duty is paid, operating under customs control.

questions.question: A warehouse warrant is a document that

options.A: Gives permission to build a warehouse
options.B: Proves ownership of goods stored in a public warehouse and can be used as security for a loan
options.C: Certifies the quality of goods stored
options.D: Shows the customs duty paid on imported goods

questions.explanation: A warehouse warrant is a receipt issued by the warehouse owner proving ownership of stored goods. It can be used as collateral to obtain a bank loan.

questions.question: Cold storage warehouses are specifically designed for

options.A: Storing flammable materials at low temperatures
options.B: Storing perishable goods such as fruits, vegetables, meat, and dairy products
options.C: Storing imported electronics and machinery
options.D: Storing goods in transit at seaports

questions.explanation: Cold storage warehouses are equipped with refrigeration to preserve perishable goods (food items, pharmaceuticals, flowers) and extend their shelf life.

questions.question: The main aim of advertising is to

options.A: Generate revenue for the media industry
options.B: Inform, persuade, and remind consumers to buy goods or services
options.C: Reduce the prices of goods in the market
options.D: Regulate business activities in the country

questions.explanation: Advertising serves three key purposes: informing potential customers about products, persuading them to buy, and reminding existing customers to continue buying.

questions.question: Informative advertising is mainly used when

options.A: A product faces heavy competition
options.B: A new product is being introduced to the market
options.C: A company wants to increase brand loyalty
options.D: A product is about to be withdrawn

questions.explanation: Informative advertising is used primarily at the introduction stage of a product's life cycle to create awareness about a new product.

questions.question: Generic (collective) advertising is directed at

options.A: Promoting one specific company's brand
options.B: Promoting an entire industry or product category rather than a specific brand
options.C: Advertising to a specific demographic group
options.D: Advertising products that are generic and have no brand name

questions.explanation: Generic advertising promotes an entire industry or category (e.g., 'Drink more milk'), often funded jointly by industry associations.

questions.question: An advertising medium refers to

options.A: The content of the advertisement
options.B: The channel through which an advertisement is communicated to the target audience
options.C: The cost of producing an advertisement
options.D: The agency that creates advertisements

questions.explanation: An advertising medium is the channel used to deliver the advertising message — examples include television, radio, newspapers, billboards, and social media.

questions.question: Point-of-sale advertising includes

options.A: Television commercials
options.B: Newspaper advertisements
options.C: Window displays, posters, and handbills at the retail outlet
options.D: Radio jingles

questions.explanation: Point-of-sale (POS) advertising is carried out at the place where goods are sold, including window displays, shelf displays, posters, and handbills.

questions.question: Below-the-line advertising refers to

options.A: Advertising in print media such as newspapers
options.B: Non-media promotional activities such as sales promotions, direct mail, and exhibitions
options.C: Advertising on television and radio broadcasts
options.D: Advertising on billboards and outdoor hoardings

questions.explanation: Below-the-line (BTL) advertising refers to promotional activities that do not use paid mass media — including sales promotions, direct marketing, and trade fairs.

questions.question: A sole trader business is one that is

options.A: Owned and controlled by one person who bears all the risk
options.B: Owned by one person but managed by a board of directors
options.C: A company with only one product line
options.D: A government-owned business with one outlet

questions.explanation: A sole trader (sole proprietorship) is owned and operated by one individual who provides all capital, makes all decisions, and has unlimited liability for all debts.

questions.question: The main DISADVANTAGE of a sole trader business is

options.A: Ease of formation
options.B: Unlimited liability — personal assets can be used to pay business debts
options.C: Complete control over business decisions
options.D: Maximum confidentiality of business information

questions.explanation: The main disadvantage of a sole trader is unlimited liability — the owner is personally responsible for all business debts including with personal assets.

questions.question: A sleeping (dormant) partner is one who

options.A: Manages the business but does not invest capital
options.B: Invests capital but takes no part in management
options.C: Handles all financial matters of the partnership
options.D: Joins the partnership temporarily for a specific project

questions.explanation: A dormant partner contributes capital and shares profits/losses but does not participate in day-to-day management.

questions.question: A limited partner in a partnership has

options.A: Unlimited liability for all business debts
options.B: Liability limited only to the amount of capital invested
options.C: The right to manage and represent the partnership
options.D: Priority over other partners in profit sharing

questions.explanation: A limited partner's liability is restricted to the amount invested. They cannot participate in management. There must always be at least one general partner with unlimited liability.

questions.question: The maximum number of partners allowed in a general business partnership in most jurisdictions is
questions.explanation: In most countries, the maximum number of partners in an ordinary partnership is 20. The minimum is 2.

questions.question: In the absence of a partnership agreement, profits are shared among partners

options.A: In proportion to capital contributed
options.B: Equally among all partners
options.C: Based on seniority and experience
options.D: As decided by the most senior partner

questions.explanation: Under the Partnership Act, in the absence of an agreement, profits and losses are shared equally among all partners.

questions.question: The memorandum of association of a company sets out

options.A: The internal rules governing management of the company
options.B: The company's name, registered address, objectives, and capital, defining its relationship with the outside world
options.C: The salaries of all company directors
options.D: The profit-sharing formula for shareholders

questions.explanation: The memorandum of association is an external document defining the company's relationship with the outside world — covering name, registered office, objectives, and authorised capital.

questions.question: The articles of association of a company govern

options.A: The company's external dealings with the public
options.B: The internal management and administration including directors' powers and meeting procedures
options.C: The company's relationship with its suppliers
options.D: The tax obligations of the company

questions.explanation: The articles of association are internal rules regulating how the company is managed — directors' powers, voting rights, dividend policy, share transfers, and meeting procedures.

questions.question: A public limited company (PLC) differs from a private limited company in that

options.A: A PLC has unlimited liability
options.B: A PLC can offer its shares to the general public through a stock exchange
options.C: A private company must have a minimum of 50 shareholders
options.D: A PLC does not require a memorandum of association

questions.explanation: A public limited company can offer shares to the general public and have shares listed on a stock exchange. A private company cannot offer shares to the public.

questions.question: A debenture is

options.A: A share issued to founders of a company
options.B: A long-term loan raised by a company on which it pays a fixed rate of interest
options.C: A dividend paid to preference shareholders
options.D: A government bond issued to fund infrastructure

questions.explanation: A debenture is a fixed-interest debt instrument (loan certificate) issued by a company to raise long-term finance. Debenture holders are creditors, not shareholders.

questions.question: Preference shareholders have priority over ordinary shareholders regarding

options.A: Voting rights at company general meetings
options.B: Payment of dividends and repayment of capital in a winding up
options.C: Electing the board of directors
options.D: Access to company financial reports

questions.explanation: Preference shareholders receive their fixed dividend before ordinary shareholders and are repaid their capital before ordinary shareholders in liquidation.

questions.question: A prospectus is a document issued by a public limited company to

options.A: Register the company with the Corporate Affairs Commission
options.B: Invite members of the public to subscribe for its shares or debentures
options.C: Inform shareholders of proposed dividend payments
options.D: Notify creditors of the company's financial position

questions.explanation: A prospectus is issued when a public company offers shares or debentures to the public for the first time. It contains details about the company, its finances, and the terms of the offer.

questions.question: A certificate of incorporation is significant because

options.A: It gives a company permission to open a bank account
options.B: It legally brings the company into existence as a separate legal entity
options.C: It authorises a company to issue shares to the public
options.D: It grants tax exemption to a newly formed company

questions.explanation: A certificate of incorporation marks the legal birth of the company as a separate legal entity, distinct from its owners.

questions.question: Authorised share capital refers to

options.A: The total shares actually issued and sold to shareholders
options.B: The maximum share capital a company is allowed to issue as stated in its memorandum
options.C: The shares retained by the founders of a company
options.D: The capital contributed by preference shareholders only

questions.explanation: Authorised (nominal) share capital is the maximum amount of share capital permitted to be issued, as stated in the memorandum of association. It may differ from issued capital.

questions.question: Ordinary shares are also known as

options.A: Preference shares
options.B: Debentures
options.C: Equity shares
options.D: Bearer shares

questions.explanation: Ordinary shares are also called equity shares. Ordinary shareholders are the residual owners, sharing in profits after all other claims but bearing the highest risk.

questions.question: Dividends paid to shareholders come from

options.A: The company's bank loans
options.B: The company's profits after tax
options.C: The company's share capital
options.D: Government subsidies to the company

questions.explanation: Dividends are distributions of a company's after-tax profits to shareholders, recommended by the board and approved at the Annual General Meeting (AGM).

questions.question: A holding (parent) company is one that

options.A: Provides warehousing facilities for other companies
options.B: Owns sufficient shares in one or more other companies to control them
options.C: Holds all shares in trust for the shareholders
options.D: Manages the day-to-day operations of a group of companies

questions.explanation: A holding company acquires a controlling interest (usually more than 50% of shares) in other companies (subsidiaries), allowing it to control their management.

questions.question: Horizontal integration occurs when

options.A: A company takes over a supplier of its raw materials
options.B: A company merges with another at the same stage of production in the same industry
options.C: A company diversifies into an entirely different industry
options.D: A small company sells shares to the public for the first time

questions.explanation: Horizontal integration is the merger or takeover of firms at the same stage of production in the same industry — it reduces competition and achieves economies of scale.

questions.question: Vertical integration occurs when

options.A: A company merges with another at the same stage of production
options.B: A company expands by acquiring businesses at different stages of its production or distribution chain
options.C: A company diversifies into unrelated industries
options.D: Two competing companies agree to set the same prices

questions.explanation: Vertical integration involves acquiring firms at different stages of the production/distribution process, helping reduce costs and secure supply or distribution channels.

questions.question: The Rochdale Principles are associated with the cooperative movement because

options.A: They outline key principles established by the Rochdale Pioneers in 1844
options.B: They were the first laws governing cooperatives enacted in Rochdale
options.C: They describe how cooperative banks should operate
options.D: They set the taxation rules for cooperatives worldwide

questions.explanation: The Rochdale Pioneers (1844) established the first successful consumer cooperative. Their principles — open membership, democratic control (one member, one vote), surplus distribution — became the foundation of the global cooperative movement.

questions.question: Which of the following is a feature of a cooperative society?

options.A: Profit is the primary motive
options.B: Members have one vote regardless of the number of shares held
options.C: Only wealthy individuals can become members
options.D: Shares can be freely transferred to non-members

questions.explanation: Democratic control — one member, one vote — is a key cooperative principle, regardless of the number of shares held. This distinguishes cooperatives from companies.

questions.question: A public corporation is an enterprise that is

options.A: Owned by private shareholders and listed on the stock exchange
options.B: Owned and operated by the government to provide essential services
options.C: A partnership between the government and private investors
options.D: A cooperative society sponsored by the government

questions.explanation: A public corporation (statutory corporation) is wholly owned and controlled by the government, set up by an Act of Parliament to provide essential services such as electricity and water.

questions.question: Privatisation refers to the process of

options.A: Transferring ownership of government enterprises to private individuals or companies
options.B: Government taking over private businesses to provide essential services
options.C: Converting a private company into a cooperative society
options.D: Restricting foreign companies from operating in a country

questions.explanation: Privatisation transfers ownership and/or control of state-owned enterprises to the private sector, aiming to improve efficiency, reduce government expenditure, and attract private investment.

questions.question: Which of the following is a primary function of money?

options.A: Standard of deferred payment
options.B: Medium of exchange
options.C: Store of value
options.D: Unit of account

questions.explanation: The most essential primary function of money is as a medium of exchange — eliminating the need for barter by acting as a universally accepted means of payment.

questions.question: The problem of double coincidence of wants is associated with

options.A: A monetary economy
options.B: A barter economy
options.C: A credit economy
options.D: An e-commerce economy

questions.explanation: In a barter economy, trade requires each party to want exactly what the other has. Money eliminates this problem by acting as a universally accepted medium of exchange.

questions.question: Token money refers to

options.A: Money whose face value equals its intrinsic value
options.B: Money whose face value exceeds the value of the material from which it is made
options.C: Foreign currency used in international trade
options.D: Electronic money used only for online transactions

questions.explanation: Token money (e.g., coins) has a face value exceeding its intrinsic (material) worth. A coin is worth more as currency than the metal it is made of.

questions.question: Inflation is defined as

options.A: A decrease in the general price level over time
options.B: A sustained increase in the general price level over a period of time
options.C: An increase in a country's money supply by the central bank
options.D: A decrease in the purchasing power of foreign currency

questions.explanation: Inflation is a sustained and general rise in the price level over time, reducing the purchasing power of money. It is measured by price indices such as the Consumer Price Index (CPI).

questions.question: Trade discount is

options.A: A reduction given to a buyer who pays cash immediately
options.B: A reduction in the list price given by a seller, usually to a bulk purchaser or fellow trader
options.C: Interest charged on goods sold on credit
options.D: A bonus given to loyal customers at year end

questions.explanation: Trade discount is a reduction from the published (list) price given to buyers in the same trade or for bulk purchases, deducted before the invoice is prepared.

questions.question: Cash discount is offered to encourage

options.A: Buyers to purchase in large quantities
options.B: Buyers to pay their debts promptly within a stipulated period
options.C: Retailers to stock more of a product
options.D: Customers to visit the store during off-peak periods

questions.explanation: Cash discount is a reduction given as an incentive for prompt payment — e.g., '2/10 net 30' means a 2% discount if paid within 10 days.

questions.question: The term 'COD' in a business transaction means

options.A: Credit on Delivery
options.B: Cash on Delivery
options.C: Cheque on Demand
options.D: Collect on Despatch

questions.explanation: COD stands for Cash on Delivery — payment is made by the buyer when goods are delivered, eliminating credit risk for the seller.

questions.question: Under hire purchase, ownership of goods

options.A: Passes to the buyer immediately on taking possession
options.B: Remains with the seller until the final instalment is paid
options.C: Passes to the buyer after the first payment
options.D: Is shared equally between buyer and seller

questions.explanation: Under hire purchase, the buyer takes possession immediately but ownership only transfers when the final payment is made.

questions.question: A mortgage is a loan that is

options.A: Secured against property (real estate)
options.B: Given by the central bank to commercial banks
options.C: A short-term overdraft facility for businesses
options.D: A loan given to the government by international donors

questions.explanation: A mortgage is a long-term loan secured by property. If the borrower defaults, the lender has the right to sell the property to recover the loan.

questions.question: An import licence is a document that

options.A: Certifies the quality of imported goods
options.B: Grants official permission to import specified goods into a country
options.C: Shows the customs duty paid on imported goods
options.D: Insures goods during transit

questions.explanation: An import licence is an official government document authorising the import of specified goods. It is a trade restriction tool used to control the volume, type, or source of imports.

questions.question: A certificate of origin is issued to

options.A: Show the insurance value of exported goods
options.B: Certify the country in which the exported goods were produced or manufactured
options.C: Show the route goods will take to reach the destination
options.D: Confirm that customs duties have been paid

questions.explanation: A certificate of origin certifies the country where goods were manufactured, used by customs authorities to determine applicable tariff rates.

questions.question: FOB (Free On Board) in international trade means

options.A: The seller bears all costs and risks until goods are delivered to the buyer's door
options.B: The seller's responsibility ends once goods are loaded onto the ship
options.C: The buyer pays for freight from the seller's warehouse to the port
options.D: All costs are shared equally between buyer and seller

questions.explanation: Under FOB terms, the seller delivers goods to the named port and loads them. Once loaded, risk and costs transfer to the buyer who arranges freight and insurance.

questions.question: CIF (Cost, Insurance, and Freight) means the seller

options.A: Only pays the cost of production
options.B: Pays for the cost of goods, insurance, and freight to the named destination port
options.C: Pays only for freight to the port of shipment
options.D: Bears all costs until goods are delivered to the buyer's premises

questions.explanation: Under CIF terms, the seller pays for the cost of goods, marine insurance, and freight to the buyer's named destination port.

questions.question: A consular invoice is used in international trade to

options.A: Record goods stored in a warehouse
options.B: Certify goods for customs purposes in the importing country
options.C: Insure goods during shipment
options.D: Request payment from the buyer's bank

questions.explanation: A consular invoice is certified by the consul of the importing country and used by customs authorities to verify origin, quantity, and value of goods for customs assessment.

questions.question: In commerce, a market refers to

options.A: Only a physical place where buyers and sellers meet
options.B: Any arrangement by which buyers and sellers are brought into contact to exchange goods and services
options.C: A government-controlled distribution centre
options.D: A place where only agricultural produce is sold

questions.explanation: A market in commerce is not necessarily a physical place — it is any arrangement (including telephone or internet) where buyers and sellers interact to exchange goods or services.

questions.question: A commodity exchange is a market where

options.A: Finished consumer goods are sold directly to consumers
options.B: Standardised raw materials and primary products are bought and sold in large quantities
options.C: Shares of companies are listed and traded
options.D: Foreign currencies are exchanged

questions.explanation: A commodity exchange is an organised market for trading standardised raw materials or primary commodities (oil, cocoa, coffee, gold) often through futures contracts.

questions.question: The stock exchange performs which of the following functions?

options.A: It provides a market for buying and selling company shares and government bonds
options.B: It controls the prices of goods in the economy
options.C: It provides loans to businesses and individuals
options.D: It manufactures and distributes national currency

questions.explanation: The stock exchange is an organised market for the buying and selling of previously issued securities — company shares, government bonds, and debentures.

questions.question: The money market deals primarily in

options.A: Long-term financial instruments such as shares and debentures
options.B: Short-term financial instruments such as Treasury bills and commercial paper
options.C: Foreign currency exchanges between countries
options.D: Physical commodities such as gold and crude oil

questions.explanation: The money market is a market for short-term financial instruments with maturities of one year or less.

questions.question: The capital market deals in

options.A: Short-term credit instruments maturing in less than one year
options.B: Long-term financial instruments such as shares and bonds
options.C: Foreign exchange transactions
options.D: Physical commodities at the commodity exchange

questions.explanation: The capital market is a market for long-term financial instruments — shares (equities) and bonds/debentures — used to raise long-term capital.

questions.question: A stockbroker's primary role in the stock exchange is to

options.A: Issue new shares on behalf of companies
options.B: Buy and sell securities on behalf of clients for a commission
options.C: Regulate the activities of the stock exchange
options.D: Set the prices at which shares are bought and sold

questions.explanation: A stockbroker is a licensed professional who executes buy and sell orders for securities on behalf of investors, earning a commission on each transaction.

questions.question: A bull in the stock market is an investor who

options.A: Sells shares expecting prices to fall so they can buy back cheaper
options.B: Buys shares expecting prices to rise so they can be sold at a profit
options.C: Holds shares indefinitely without selling
options.D: Buys shares in all companies regardless of performance

questions.explanation: A bull is an optimistic investor who buys securities expecting their prices to rise. A bear does the opposite — selling in expectation of falling prices.

questions.question: The balance of payments of a country records

options.A: All revenues and expenditures of the government during a year
options.B: All financial transactions between the country and the rest of the world over a period
options.C: The total value of goods produced in the country in a year
options.D: The assets and liabilities of the central bank

questions.explanation: The balance of payments (BOP) is a systematic record of all economic transactions between a country's residents and the rest of the world over a given period.

questions.question: Which of the following would be recorded in the current account of a country's balance of payments?

options.A: Foreign direct investment flows
options.B: Purchases of government bonds by foreign investors
options.C: Export and import of goods and services
options.D: Loans from the International Monetary Fund

questions.explanation: The current account records trade in goods (visible trade), trade in services (invisible trade), income (wages, dividends), and current transfers (remittances).

questions.question: The Consumer Protection Council (CPC) in Nigeria was established to

options.A: Regulate imports and exports
options.B: Protect consumers from unfair trade practices, substandard goods, and false advertising
options.C: Set prices for essential goods in the market
options.D: Collect taxes on behalf of the government

questions.explanation: The CPC safeguards consumers against unsafe products, deceptive practices, and exploitation, ensuring consumer rights are upheld.

questions.question: The phrase 'caveat emptor' means

options.A: Let the seller beware
options.B: Let the buyer beware
options.C: The buyer is always right
options.D: Both buyer and seller share responsibility

questions.explanation: 'Caveat emptor' (Latin) means 'let the buyer beware.' It placed responsibility on buyers to check quality before purchase. Consumer protection laws have largely replaced this doctrine.

questions.question: NAFDAC in Nigeria is primarily responsible for

options.A: Controlling the prices of pharmaceuticals
options.B: Regulating and controlling the manufacture, importation, and sale of food, drugs, and cosmetics
options.C: Setting standards for industrial goods and electronics
options.D: Protecting companies from patent infringement

questions.explanation: NAFDAC (National Agency for Food and Drug Administration and Control) regulates the manufacture, importation, distribution, and sale of food, drugs, cosmetics, and medical devices.

questions.question: Tertiary production refers to

options.A: The extraction of natural resources from the earth
options.B: The transformation of raw materials into finished products
options.C: The provision of services that support and distribute goods
options.D: The cultivation of agricultural products

questions.explanation: Tertiary production (the service sector) includes commerce (trade, banking, insurance, transport, communication) and direct services (education, healthcare, entertainment).

questions.question: Division of labour refers to

options.A: Allocating land resources between agricultural and industrial uses
options.B: Breaking down the production process into specialised tasks with each worker performing one task
options.C: Dividing a country's workforce between public and private sectors
options.D: Classifying workers into skilled and unskilled categories

questions.explanation: Division of labour involves splitting the production process into specialised tasks, with each worker concentrating on one task, increasing productivity, skill, and efficiency.

questions.question: Working capital is defined as

options.A: The total assets of a business
options.B: Current assets minus current liabilities
options.C: Fixed assets minus long-term liabilities
options.D: Total revenue minus total costs

questions.explanation: Working capital = Current Assets – Current Liabilities. It measures a business's short-term liquidity and ability to meet day-to-day obligations.

questions.question: A fixed asset is best described as

options.A: Cash and bank balances held by a business
options.B: An asset purchased for long-term use in the business, not for resale
options.C: Stock of goods available for sale
options.D: Money owed to the business by debtors

questions.explanation: Fixed assets (non-current assets) are long-term assets used in operating the business — not intended for sale — such as land, buildings, machinery, and vehicles.

questions.question: Depreciation of a fixed asset refers to

options.A: The physical deterioration of the asset only
options.B: The reduction in value of a fixed asset due to wear, tear, obsolescence, or passage of time
options.C: The increase in the value of an asset due to inflation
options.D: The cost of repairing and maintaining a fixed asset

questions.explanation: Depreciation is the systematic allocation of the cost of a fixed asset over its useful life, reflecting reduction in value due to wear, usage, and obsolescence.

questions.question: The break-even point in business is the level of output at which

options.A: Profit is at its maximum
options.B: Total revenue equals total cost — neither profit nor loss is made
options.C: Variable costs equal fixed costs
options.D: The business starts to make a loss

questions.explanation: The break-even point is where total revenue equals total costs. Below this point the business makes a loss; above it, a profit.

questions.question: Fixed costs in a business are

options.A: Costs that vary directly with the level of output
options.B: Costs that remain constant regardless of the level of production
options.C: Costs incurred only when a new product is launched
options.D: Costs that can be easily changed in the short run

questions.explanation: Fixed costs remain constant regardless of output level — examples include rent, insurance, salaries of permanent staff, and depreciation.

questions.question: Variable costs in a business are

options.A: Costs that remain the same regardless of production level
options.B: Costs that change in direct proportion to the level of output
options.C: One-off costs incurred at the start of a business
options.D: Costs that are only incurred when the business makes a profit

questions.explanation: Variable costs change in direct proportion to production — examples include raw materials, direct labour, and packaging.

questions.question: If a trader buys goods for ₦8,000 and sells them for ₦10,000, the percentage profit on cost is
A trader bought goods for ₦5,000 and sold them for ₦4,500. The percentage loss on cost is
Simple interest on ₦20,000 at 5% per annum for 3 years is
A 20% trade discount on goods listed at ₦15,000 gives a discount of
If goods are bought for ₦12,000 and sold at a mark-up of 25%, the selling price is
A trader sells goods for ₦18,000, making a profit of 20% on the selling price. The cost price is
A company's total assets are ₦500,000 and total liabilities are ₦300,000. The owner's equity is
questions.explanation: Owner's Equity = Total Assets – Total Liabilities = ₦500,000 – ₦300,000 = ₦200,000 (accounting equation).

questions.question: Which of the following organisations was established to promote free trade among nations?

options.A: International Monetary Fund (IMF)
options.B: World Trade Organisation (WTO)
options.C: World Bank
options.D: UNCTAD

questions.explanation: The WTO (established 1995) promotes and regulates international trade by negotiating agreements, resolving disputes, and reducing trade barriers.

questions.question: ECOWAS stands for

options.A: Economic Community of West African States
options.B: East and Central Organisation of West African Suppliers
options.C: Economic and Commercial Organisation for West African States
options.D: European Community of West African States

questions.explanation: ECOWAS (Economic Community of West African States) is a regional organisation of 15 West African countries established in 1975 to promote economic integration and free trade.

questions.question: The African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) was established to

options.A: Create a single currency for all African countries
options.B: Create a single African market for goods and services and promote intra-African trade
options.C: Regulate foreign direct investment in Africa
options.D: Provide financial aid to African countries

questions.explanation: The AfCFTA (operational 2021) aims to create a continent-wide free trade area by reducing tariffs and barriers among African countries.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes e-commerce?

options.A: Commerce conducted only by government-owned enterprises
options.B: The buying and selling of goods and services over the internet
options.C: Electronic control of factory production
options.D: The use of computers for accounting purposes only

questions.explanation: E-commerce refers to commercial transactions — buying, selling, and transferring goods, services, or information — conducted electronically over the internet.

questions.question: Which of the following is a formal channel of communication in an organisation?

options.A: Gossip through colleagues
options.B: An official memo from management to staff
options.C: A conversation at the staff canteen
options.D: Text messages between co-workers

questions.explanation: Formal communication follows the official organisational hierarchy — examples include memos, official letters, reports, and board minutes.

questions.question: A trade union is an organisation formed by

options.A: Business owners to protect their trading interests
options.B: Workers in the same or similar industries to protect and promote their employment interests
options.C: The government to regulate wages in the economy
options.D: Employers to agree on minimum wage rates

questions.explanation: A trade union is a collective organisation of workers formed to negotiate better wages, working conditions, job security, and other employment benefits.

questions.question: The minimum wage is the

options.A: Highest wage that employers are permitted to pay workers
options.B: Lowest wage that employers are legally required to pay their workers
options.C: Average wage paid in an industry as set by the trade union
options.D: Wage paid to government workers by the treasury

questions.explanation: The minimum wage is the lowest legal wage rate employers are permitted to pay, set by law to ensure workers can meet basic living standards.

questions.question: In a monopoly market, there is

options.A: Many sellers producing identical goods
options.B: A single seller dominating the entire market supply
options.C: A few large sellers controlling the market
options.D: Many sellers producing slightly differentiated products

questions.explanation: A monopoly exists when there is only one seller in the market, giving significant control over price and supply.

questions.question: An oligopoly is a market structure characterised by

options.A: One dominant seller
options.B: Many sellers selling identical products
options.C: A few large firms dominating the market
options.D: Many firms selling differentiated products

questions.explanation: An oligopoly has a small number of large firms dominating the market (e.g., telecommunications or airline industry). Firms are interdependent.

questions.question: Perfect competition is characterised by

options.A: One seller and many buyers
options.B: A few firms with significant market power
options.C: Many buyers and sellers trading identical products with free entry and exit
options.D: Differentiated products and heavy advertising

questions.explanation: Under perfect competition: many buyers and sellers, homogeneous products, free entry and exit, perfect information, and no single firm can influence price.

questions.question: Ploughing back profits (retained earnings) as a source of finance means

options.A: Borrowing profits from the central bank
options.B: Reinvesting the company's undistributed profits back into the business
options.C: Transferring profits into a government savings account
options.D: Distributing extra profits to shareholders as dividends

questions.explanation: Retained earnings involve using undistributed profits to finance business growth. It is a cheap, internal source of finance with no issue costs.

questions.question: Trade credit is a form of finance that arises when

options.A: A business borrows money from a commercial bank
options.B: A supplier allows a buyer to receive goods now and pay at a later agreed date
options.C: A company issues bonds to the public to raise money
options.D: An entrepreneur uses personal savings to fund a business

questions.explanation: Trade credit arises when a supplier delivers goods but allows payment at a later date (e.g., 30 or 60 days). It is a short-term finance form not involving bank borrowing.

questions.question: An invoice discounting is a method of finance where

options.A: A company sells its outstanding invoices to a finance house at a discount to obtain immediate cash
options.B: A supplier gives a discount on invoices paid promptly
options.C: A bank lends money against fixed assets
options.D: A company delays payment of invoices to manage cash flow

questions.explanation: Invoice discounting (factoring) is a short-term finance method where a business sells its accounts receivable (invoices) to a financial institution at a discount, receiving immediate cash.

questions.question: A superstore differs from a supermarket mainly in that

options.A: A superstore only sells food items
options.B: A superstore is much larger and stocks a wider range of goods including non-food items
options.C: A supermarket charges higher prices than a superstore
options.D: A superstore is always located in rural areas

questions.explanation: A superstore (hypermarket) is larger than a supermarket, offering a very wide range of products including food, clothing, electronics, and household goods.

questions.question: Scrambled merchandising in retailing means

options.A: Selling goods at randomly changing prices
options.B: Selling goods from different and unrelated product categories together in one retail outlet
options.C: Mixing counterfeit goods with genuine products
options.D: Rapidly changing the arrangement of goods on shelves

questions.explanation: Scrambled merchandising occurs when a retailer stocks goods from various unrelated categories, helping increase customer traffic and revenue.

questions.question: A credit cooperative (thrift and credit society) is formed to

options.A: Market agricultural products on behalf of farmers
options.B: Provide savings and credit facilities (loans) to members at affordable interest rates
options.C: Purchase goods in bulk for resale to members
options.D: Build houses for members at subsidised rates

questions.explanation: A credit cooperative (SACCO) collects savings from members and provides affordable loans, often at lower rates than commercial banks.

questions.question: A free warehouse (free zone) in international trade is one where

options.A: Goods are stored free of charge for the owner
options.B: Goods can be stored and handled without payment of import duty while in storage
options.C: The government provides warehouse services at no cost
options.D: Perishable goods are stored in refrigerated conditions for free

questions.explanation: A free zone warehouse allows goods to be stored, processed, or re-exported without payment of customs duties. Duty is only paid when goods enter the country for domestic consumption.

questions.question: A charter party agreement is used in

options.A: Air transport — hiring an entire aircraft
options.B: Sea transport — an agreement to hire an entire vessel or part of it
options.C: Road transport — chartering a fleet of trucks
options.D: Rail transport — hiring a special train

questions.explanation: A charter party is a contract between the shipowner and the charterer for the hire of a whole vessel or part of it for a voyage or time period.

questions.question: Cabotage law in shipping refers to

options.A: The right of a country to control air traffic over its territory
options.B: A regulation requiring that trade between domestic ports be carried only by ships registered in that country
options.C: International rules governing the safety of passenger ships
options.D: A treaty that regulates fishing in international waters

questions.explanation: Cabotage laws restrict transport of goods between domestic ports to ships that are registered in and owned by nationals of the same country, protecting domestic shipping industries.

questions.question: A rights issue refers to

options.A: New shares offered to the general public at a discount
options.B: New shares offered to existing shareholders in proportion to their current holdings
options.C: Shares given free to employees as a bonus
options.D: Shares repurchased by the company from shareholders

questions.explanation: A rights issue gives existing shareholders the right to buy new shares at a discounted price in proportion to their existing shareholding.

questions.question: The winding up of a company means

options.A: Expanding the company's operations to new territories
options.B: The process of closing down a company and distributing its assets to settle debts
options.C: Transferring the company to new shareholders
options.D: Converting a public company to a private company

questions.explanation: Winding up (liquidation) is the legal process of closing a company — selling assets, paying debts, and distributing remaining funds to shareholders.

questions.question: An Annual General Meeting (AGM) is a meeting where

options.A: The board of directors meets to decide on company strategy
options.B: Shareholders meet at least once a year to receive reports, approve accounts, declare dividends, and elect directors
options.C: Employees meet to discuss working conditions
options.D: Creditors meet to decide on debt repayment plans

questions.explanation: At an AGM, directors present annual accounts, dividends are declared, directors may be elected or re-elected, and shareholders vote on major resolutions.

questions.question: Sponsorship as a form of promotion involves

options.A: Paying for commercial airtime on television
options.B: A company funding events, sports teams, or individuals in exchange for brand visibility
options.C: Giving free samples of goods to potential customers
options.D: Paying retailers to prominently display a product

questions.explanation: Sponsorship involves a company providing financial support for events, sports, arts, or charities in return for brand exposure and positive association.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST represents the scope of commerce?

options.A: Trade only
options.B: Trade and all aids to trade
options.C: Production, trade, and consumption
options.D: Distribution of goods only

questions.explanation: Commerce comprises trade (buying and selling) and aids to trade (banking, insurance, transportation, warehousing, advertising, communication). Production is industry, not commerce.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of secondary production?

options.A: Fishing
options.B: Teaching
options.C: Manufacturing cars from steel
options.D: Transporting goods by truck

questions.explanation: Secondary production involves transforming raw materials into finished goods through manufacturing. Fishing is primary; teaching and transport are tertiary.

questions.question: Venture capital is best described as

options.A: Government grants given to agricultural cooperatives
options.B: Risky investment capital provided to start-up businesses with high growth potential
options.C: Loans provided by commercial banks at very low interest rates
options.D: Capital saved by individuals in fixed deposit accounts

questions.explanation: Venture capital is risk investment provided to fund start-up or early-stage businesses with high growth potential. The investor typically takes an equity stake.

questions.question: Near money (quasi-money) refers to

options.A: Counterfeit currency produced illegally
options.B: Highly liquid assets that can quickly and easily be converted to cash
options.C: Foreign currency held by a central bank
options.D: Money borrowed from the IMF

questions.explanation: Near money includes highly liquid financial assets that can be quickly converted to cash without significant loss of value — e.g., Treasury bills, savings deposits.

questions.question: A voluntary chain (voluntary group) in the distribution system is

options.A: A group of independent retailers who group together to purchase from the same wholesaler
options.B: A group of manufacturers who combine to sell through one agent
options.C: A group of wholesalers who merge to form one large company
options.D: A government-sponsored supply chain for essential goods

questions.explanation: A voluntary chain is an arrangement where independent retailers group together to purchase from a single wholesaler, gaining economies of scale while remaining independent.

questions.question: A futures market is one in which

options.A: Goods are bought and paid for immediately for future delivery at today's agreed price
options.B: Goods are sold only to consumers in the future
options.C: Prices are set by government for future years
options.D: Companies list their shares for the first time

questions.explanation: In a futures market, contracts are made to buy or sell a commodity at an agreed price with delivery and payment at a specified future date, helping hedge against price fluctuations.

questions.question: A consignment note is a document used in

options.A: Sea transport — listing goods loaded onto a ship
options.B: Road and rail transport — listing goods handed to a carrier for delivery
options.C: Air transport — describing goods packed in a container
options.D: International trade — certifying the value of exported goods
